text: This is a young female who was admitted with acute cholecystitis the night before.
The video shows a contracted gallbladder encased in a layer of surrounding fat, as revealed by ultrasound imaging.
The surgeon switches to indocyanine green imaging, revealing the cystic duct, which does not illuminate as expected in cases of acute cholecystitis.

Acute Coley's, I'll usually use three cc's to four cc's, sometimes a little bit more for acute ones, just to hopefully that it lights up. I try to give it between thirty and forty-five minutes before and holding.
The surgeon uses four robotic arms to perform dissection, aiming to achieve the critical view of safety during the procedure.
The robotic setup includes four arms: arm one equipped with a ProGrasp, arm two with a Maryland Dissector, arm three holding the camera, and arm four with a hook cautery.
The surgeon dissects out the cystic artery, ensuring ample space for placing three clips. The margin of the liver is visible, and the critical view of safety has been obtained.
The surgeon places clips on the cystic artery, and minor bleeding occurs at the site, which is a common but manageable occurrence during the procedure.
The surgeon uses cautery to stop the bleeding, which ceases immediately upon application.
The surgeon uses robotic arms to dissect down to the cystic plate during a standard cholecystectomy, maintaining a clear view of the anatomical structures.
